sections.title: Definite Articles (the)
sections.explanation: German has three definite articles corresponding to 'the' in English, one for each grammatical gender: 'der' (masculine), 'die' (feminine), 'das' (neuter). The plural is always 'die' for all genders. These articles agree with the noun's gender: der Mann (the man), die Frau (the woman), das Kind (the child), die Kinder (the children). The definite article signals that you are talking about a specific, known thing.
sections.tip: der = masculine, die = feminine, das = neuter, die = plural. One easy trick: think of 'die' as the 'default' for feminine and all plurals.

exercises.prompt: What is the definite article for 'Hund' (dog)?
exercises.explanation: 'Hund' (dog) is masculine: der Hund. Most living male creatures are masculine.

exercises.prompt: What is the definite article for 'Stadt' (city)?
exercises.explanation: 'Stadt' (city) is feminine: die Stadt. Most cities are feminine in German when an article is used.

exercises.prompt: ___ Kind spielt. (The child plays.) — neuter noun
exercises.explanation: 'Kind' is neuter: das Kind. Das Kind spielt = The child plays.

exercises.prompt: What is the definite article for 'Bücher' (books, plural)?
exercises.explanation: All plural nouns use 'die', regardless of their singular gender: das Buch → die Bücher.

exercises.prompt: Match each noun to its correct definite article:
exercises.explanation: der Tisch, die Lampe, das Buch, die Bücher. Gender must be memorized with each noun.

exercises.prompt: Which article is used for ALL plural nouns?
exercises.explanation: All plural nouns take 'die': die Männer, die Frauen, die Kinder, die Häuser.

exercises.prompt: ___ Katze schläft. (The cat sleeps.) — cats are feminine
exercises.explanation: 'die Katze' — Katze is feminine. Die Katze schläft = The cat sleeps.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence is correct?
exercises.explanation: 'Mann' (man) is masculine: der Mann. Der Mann ist nett = The man is nice.

sections.title: Indefinite Articles (a / an)
sections.explanation: The indefinite article ('a' or 'an' in English) in German is: 'ein' for masculine and neuter nouns, 'eine' for feminine nouns. The plural has no indefinite article (just the bare noun, or 'keine' for 'no/not any'). Examples: ein Mann (a man), eine Frau (a woman), ein Kind (a child), Männer (men). Use the indefinite article when introducing something new or unspecified. Note that 'ein' is also the number 'one'.
sections.tip: ein = masculine and neuter (both!), eine = feminine. No plural indefinite article — just use the bare noun.

examples.en: I see a dog. (accusative masculine — einen)

exercises.prompt: What is the indefinite article for 'Mann' (man, masculine)?
exercises.explanation: Masculine nouns take 'ein' in the nominative case: ein Mann (a man).

exercises.prompt: What is the indefinite article for 'Frau' (woman, feminine)?
exercises.explanation: Feminine nouns take 'eine': eine Frau (a woman).

exercises.prompt: Ich habe ___ Katze. (I have a cat.) — Katze is feminine
exercises.explanation: 'Katze' is feminine, but wait — it is the direct object here (accusative). Feminine 'eine' does not change in the accusative. Ich habe eine Katze.

exercises.prompt: What is the indefinite article for 'Buch' (book, neuter)?
exercises.explanation: Neuter nouns take 'ein' (same as masculine in nominative): ein Buch (a book).

exercises.prompt: Match each noun to its indefinite article (nominative):
exercises.explanation: ein Mann, eine Frau, ein Kind. Plural nouns have no indefinite article in German.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'a house' in German? (Haus is neuter)
exercises.explanation: 'Haus' is neuter → ein Haus. 'ein' covers both masculine and neuter in the nominative.

exercises.prompt: Das ist ___ Auto. (That is a car.) — Auto is neuter
exercises.hint: Auto is neuter — same as masculine for indefinite articles
exercises.explanation: 'Auto' is neuter → ein Auto. Das ist ein Auto = That is a car.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct plural of 'ein Hund' (a dog)?
exercises.explanation: German has no plural indefinite article. 'Ein Hund' (a dog) becomes just 'Hunde' (dogs) in the plural. You can say 'keine Hunde' (no dogs).

sections.title: Definite vs Indefinite
sections.explanation: Use the definite article (der/die/das) when both speaker and listener know which specific thing is being talked about, or when something has already been mentioned. Use the indefinite article (ein/eine) when introducing something for the first time, or when referring to any example of a category. In German, the articles also change form depending on the grammatical case (nominative, accusative, dative, genitive) — the nominative forms are learned here; other cases are covered later.
sections.tip: Definite (der/die/das) = specific, known. Indefinite (ein/eine) = new, any. 'Ich sehe einen Hund' → 'Der Hund ist groß.' First mention = ein, then refer back = der.

examples.de: Ich kaufe ein Buch. Das Buch ist interessant.
examples.en: I'm buying a book. The book is interesting.

examples.de: Eine Frau steht da. Die Frau lächelt.
examples.en: A woman is standing there. The woman is smiling.
The sun is shining. (always 'the sun' — specific)

exercises.prompt: You mention a dog for the first time. Which article do you use?
exercises.explanation: First mention uses the indefinite article: 'Ich sehe einen Hund.' After that, you refer back with 'der Hund'.

exercises.prompt: The sun is always specific. How do you say 'The sun shines'?
exercises.explanation: Unique things like the sun (die Sonne), the moon (der Mond), and proper contexts use the definite article.

exercises.prompt: Ich habe ___ Schwester. (I have a sister.) — first mention, feminine
exercises.explanation: First mention of your sister = indefinite article. Schwester is feminine → eine. Ich habe eine Schwester.

exercises.prompt: You already mentioned a book. Now you refer to it again. Which article?
exercises.explanation: After the first mention with 'ein Buch', you refer back with 'das Buch'. Definite articles indicate known, already-mentioned items.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly introduces a new car?
exercises.explanation: New information → indefinite article: 'ein Auto'. 'das Auto' would imply a specific known car.
